source.title: Tesoro Martinez Refinery Process Safety Culture Case Study - February 12, 2014 and March 10, 2014 Sulfuric Acid Release Incidents

event.top_event: Loss of Containment - Sulfuric Acid Release
actions_taken: Workers immediately went to nearby safety shower
Emergency responders transferred workers by helicopter and ambulance to UC Davis Regional Burn Center
Cal/OSHA required process shutdown from February 18, 2014 until February 28, 2014
Tesoro upgraded PPE requirements to include full acid suit and air-purifying respirator
Tesoro replaced failed tubing connector and adjacent inadequately tightened compression fitting
Tesoro commissioned Anamet, Inc. to conduct materials testing on failed tubing
event.summary: On February 12, 2014, two Tesoro Martinez refinery employees suffered first and second degree chemical burns while working to return a sulfuric acid sampling system to service following nonroutine maintenance. Concentrated sulfuric acid sprayed the workers following a mechanical integrity failure of a ¾-inch tubing connector adjacent to the acid sampling station. The tubing came apart at the connector when operators opened a valve to return the acid sampling system to service. Acid continued to spray from a nearly 100,000-gallon acid settler for over two hours, resulting in the release of approximately 84,000 pounds of sulfuric acid. Both workers were transported to a burn center and neither returned to work for over 150 days. The incident was caused by insufficient tightening of the compression joint. The CSB investigation found that weak process safety culture at the refinery, including failure to implement inherently safer design, inadequate PPE availability, poor maintenance practices, and failure to learn from previous incidents, were causal to the incident.
recommendations: Implement inherently safer sulfuric acid sampling system design with closed-loop systems
Ensure all required PPE is available and compatible for workers
Strengthen process hazard analysis to adequately evaluate sampling system hazards
Implement robust leak-testing procedures for tubing following repairs
Improve maintenance program planning, documentation, and communication
Strengthen process safety culture through management commitment and worker engagement
Implement effective corrective actions from safety culture assessments
Improve communication and implementation of lessons learned from previous incidents
Enhance regulatory oversight through proactive and preventive inspections
Use leading and lagging process safety indicators to identify impending serious incidents

hazards.name: Sulfuric Acid - Corrosive and Reactive Chemical

threats.name: Inadequate Maintenance and Leak Testing Procedures
Failure to Implement Inherently Safer Design

consequences.name: Worker Chemical Burns - First and Second Degree
Loss of Workdays - Extended Worker Absence
Large Quantity Release - 84,000 pounds of Sulfuric Acid
Potential Escalation to Fire or Explosion

controls.name: Mechanical Integrity Program - Tubing and Connector Inspection
controls.barrier_role: Prevent tubing connector failure through proper installation and inspection
controls.lod_basis: Primary engineering control to maintain containment integrity

supporting_text: The report concluded that the sulfuric acid release on February 12, 2014 resulted from the failure of a ¾-inch diameter stainless steel tubing connector that came apart due to insufficient tightening between a tube and a compression joint
Following the February 12, 2014 incident, maintenance workers also found an inadequately tightened compression fitting for an adjacent sample station
The Tesoro Martinez refinery did not require workers to leak-test tubing of this type following repairs

controls.name: Leak Testing Procedure - Post-Maintenance Tubing Verification
controls.barrier_role: Detect tubing integrity failures before system pressurization
controls.lod_basis: Secondary control to verify mechanical integrity before operation

supporting_text: Due to maintenance program deficiencies including gaps in planning, documentation, and communication, Tesoro did not effectively repair or leak-test the tubing prior to placing it back in service
The Tesoro Martinez refinery did not require workers to leak-test tubing of this type following repairs

controls.name: Process Hazard Analysis - Sampling System Risk Assessment
controls.barrier_role: Identify and evaluate hazards associated with sampling operations
controls.lod_basis: Foundational control to identify hazards and select appropriate safeguards

supporting_text: In its 2009 process hazard analysis (PHA), Tesoro evaluated the hazards of sampling sulfuric acid. Tesoro's PHA documentation indicates that the PHA Team identified the sampling hazard to have a potential severity of a lost workday injury, which they anticipated to be likely to occur within 20 years

In its 2013 PHA, Tesoro did not specifically evaluate the hazards of the sulfuric acid sample stations. Instead, a human factors checklist was used to cover the sample systems. The checklist questions did not evaluate sampling system hazards

the report from Contra Costa County's inspection following the February 12, 2014 incident concluded that neither the 2009 nor the 2012 alkylation unit PHAs adequately evaluated the hazards associated with the acid sample stations

controls.name: Inherently Safer Design - Closed-Loop Sampling System
controls.barrier_role: Eliminate or minimize worker exposure to sulfuric acid through engineering design
controls.lod_basis: Primary engineering control using hierarchy of controls to reduce hazard

supporting_text: In January 2010, the Tesoro Martinez refinery made a substantial financial investment in what the refinery asserted was a more robust, 'inherently safer' sampling system, which the refinery then failed to make operable and took out of service
On February 12, 2014, workers were putting the older, poorly designed system back into service when the incident occurred

Tesoro approved a project to install new closed loop sample systems in June 2009. Tesoro designed these systems to be a significant upgrade to the existing sample stations, with the goal of minimizing operator exposure to the sulfuric acid when sampling

controls.name: Personal Protective Equipment - Acid-Resistant Clothing and Gloves
controls.barrier_role: Protect workers from chemical burns if acid is released
controls.lod_basis: Last line of defense to minimize injury severity

supporting_text: At the time of the incident, the two employees were wearing the personal protective equipment (PPE) required by company policy to put the sample system back in service – hard hat, safety shoes, goggles, flame retardant jacket and coveralls, and gloves. This PPE was, however, insufficient to protect against an acid spray injury

Tesoro workers informed the CSB that of the four items of PPE required by Tesoro, only goggles and chemical gloves were actually worn. The workers did not use the acid suit jacket required by the procedure because Tesoro stopped carrying this item in its PPE inventory
After the February 12, 2014 incident, Tesoro upgraded the PPE requirements during acid sampling to include a full acid suit and air-purifying respirator

controls.name: Respiratory Protection - Air-Purifying Respirator
controls.barrier_role: Protect workers from inhalation of sulfur dioxide and acid vapors
controls.lod_basis: Last line of defense to minimize respiratory injury

supporting_text: Although the Tesoro Martinez refinery's acid sampling procedure warns that 'acid fumes are harmful if inhaled' and that 'extreme caution must be used to prevent irritation and/or injury from acid fumes,' the procedure did not recommend or require any respiratory protection to be worn
Tesoro operators informed the CSB that during acid sampling, their personal gas monitors routinely alarmed, indicating the presence of harmful sulfur dioxide gas
After the February 12, 2014 incident, Tesoro upgraded the PPE requirements during acid sampling to include a full acid suit and air-purifying respirator

controls.name: Safety Shower and Emergency Response
controls.barrier_role: Minimize injury severity through immediate decontamination
controls.lod_basis: Recovery control to mitigate consequences after release occurs

supporting_text: The operators immediately went to a nearby safety shower
Emergency responders transferred the workers by helicopter and ambulance to the UC Davis Regional Burn Center for evaluation and treatment of their sulfuric acid burn injuries

controls.name: Management of Change - Equipment Modifications and Workarounds
controls.barrier_role: Ensure changes to equipment and procedures are properly evaluated for safety impact
controls.lod_basis: Foundational control to prevent introduction of new hazards

supporting_text: In January 2012, a Tesoro worker was injured and transported to the hospital following an acid release incident from a failed temporary pump. The permanently installed pump in this service was not operable. Rather than repairing the permanent pump, Tesoro opted to install a temporary pump that was not compatible with sulfuric acid as a work-around. According to its incident investigation records, Tesoro installed this temporary pump without a Management of Change (MOC)
Reliance on inadequate temporary alkylation unit equipment or other workarounds

controls.name: Maintenance Program - Planning, Documentation, and Communication
controls.barrier_role: Ensure maintenance work is properly planned and executed to maintain equipment integrity
controls.lod_basis: Foundational control to ensure proper execution of maintenance activities

supporting_text: Due to maintenance program deficiencies including gaps in planning, documentation, and communication, Tesoro did not effectively repair or leak-test the tubing prior to placing it back in service
The sample system involved in the February 12, 2014 incident was out of service for maintenance beginning November 26, 2013 due to leaking tubing and an improperly manufactured sample container

controls.name: Safety Culture and Management Commitment
controls.barrier_role: Establish organizational commitment to prioritize safety over production
controls.lod_basis: Foundational organizational control to support all other safety systems

supporting_text: A strong process safety culture is necessary to help prevent process safety incidents and worker injuries. The safety culture at the Tesoro Martinez refinery created conditions conducive to the occurrence and recurrence of process safety incidents that caused worker injuries at the refinery over several years

Despite the serious nature of the employee injuries and quantity of hazardous chemicals released from the loss of containment, Tesoro challenged the CSB's authority to investigate the incident and failed to preserve key evidence. Tesoro corporate officials characterized the incident as a minor personal safety event rather than a process safety incident
Minimization of the seriousness of the February 12, 2014 process safety incident involving chemical burns to workers and a loss of primary containment

controls.name: Lessons Learned Program - Communication and Implementation
controls.barrier_role: Prevent recurrence of similar incidents by effectively communicating and implementing lessons from past accidents
controls.lod_basis: Foundational control to prevent recurrence of known hazards

supporting_text: The CSB found that this second incident shared similar causation with a 1999 incident at the same refinery, then called the Avon refinery, owned by the Tosco Corporation, that resulted in four fatalities. Similarities between the two incidents suggest that the Tesoro Martinez refinery did not effectively continue to implement or communicate important safety lessons from the 1999 Tosco incident
Ineffective incorporation and communication of lessons learned from previous safety incidents

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
